heading: Civil rights and elective franchise
label: 1343 Civil rights and elective franchise
notes: Historical and Revision NotesBased on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., § 41(12), (13), and (14) (, pars. 12, 13, 14, ).Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 2436 Stat. 1092Words “civil action” were substituted for “suits,” “suits at law or in equity” in view of Rule 2 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.Numerous changes were made in arrangement and phraseology.

Amendments1979— designated existing provisions as subsec. (a) and added subsec. (b).Pub. L. 96–1701957— inserted “and elective franchise” in section catchline and added par. (4).Pub. L. 85–3151954—Act , substituted “” for “” wherever appearing.Sept. 3, 1954section 1985 of Title 42section 47 of Title 8

Effective Date of 1979 Amendment, , , provided that:Pub. L. 96–170, § 393 Stat. 1284Dec. 29, 1979“The amendments made by this Act [amending this section and , The Public Health and Welfare] shall apply with respect to any deprivation of rights, privileges, or immunities secured by the Constitution and laws occurring after the date of the enactment of this Act [].”section 1983 of Title 42Dec. 29, 1979

children.text: To recover damages for injury to his person or property, or because of the deprivation of any right or privilege of a citizen of the United States, by any act done in furtherance of any conspiracy mentioned in ;section 1985 of Title 42
To recover damages from any person who fails to prevent or to aid in preventing any wrongs mentioned in which he had knowledge were about to occur and power to prevent;section 1985 of Title 42

To redress the deprivation, under color of any State law, statute, ordinance, regulation, custom or usage, of any right, privilege or immunity secured by the Constitution of the United States or by any Act of Congress providing for equal rights of citizens or of all persons within the jurisdiction of the United States;
To recover damages or to secure equitable or other relief under any Act of Congress providing for the protection of civil rights, including the right to vote.
the District of Columbia shall be considered to be a State; and
any Act of Congress applicable exclusively to the District of Columbia shall be considered to be a statute of the District of Columbia.
